nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to eastern and northeastern Brazil, where it occupies subtropical and tropical dry and moist shrubland, savanna, freshwater marshes, ponds, and aquaculture ponds. The species is almost entirely aquatic and generally ventures onto land only to escape a pool that is drying up.
nativeRange.biotope: Lowland Atlantic Forest / Caatinga ponds, marshes, and slow-moving shallow freshwater bodies of eastern Brazil.

decorPreferences: fine sand or bare bottom (avoid coarse gravel — impaction risk during feeding strikes)
broad-leaved or floating plants for cover
tightly-fitting lid (capable of brief overland movement during heavy rain or to escape drying conditions)

diet.notes: Carvalho's Surinam Toad is a suction-feeding ambush predator, as are all Pipa. It lacks both teeth and a tongue, capturing prey instead through a rapid expansion of the mouth that pulls food in with the inrushing water. Blackworms, earthworms, bloodworms, small krill, and small frozen or feeder fish are generally advised. Like all pipids, it will attempt to swallow anything that fits in its mouth, including small tankmates.

goodWith: conspecifics of similar size (in adequately sized tanks)
avoidWith: African Dwarf Frogs and other smaller pipids

summary: Carvalho's Surinam Toad is a small, fully aquatic Brazilian pipid frog, notable for the family's signature dorsal-skin brooding strategy — though uniquely among Pipa, the eggs hatch as free-swimming tadpoles rather than as fully-formed froglets.
careNotes: Carvalho's Surinam Toad is a fully aquatic, dwarf-bodied pipid — males reach around 4.4–5.7 cm and females around 6.5–6.8 cm — placing it intermediate in size between the much larger Pipa pipa and the smaller Pipa parva. It is occasionally captive-bred in the European and North American hobby but is not a routine commercial species; the closely related Pipa parva is more commonly encountered in the trade. Setup mirrors other aquatic Pipa: a long, low aquarium with a securely fitting lid (they can move overland briefly in heavy rain or to escape drying pools), gentle filtration with minimal flow, dim diffused lighting, and a fine sand or bare-bottom substrate to avoid impaction during the species' rapid suction-feeding strikes. Water is generally kept warm (around 24–27°C) and on the soft, slightly acidic-to-neutral side; their thin pipid skin is sensitive to chlorine, copper, and untested medications, so dechlorinated water and conservative quarantine are advised. Males have been reported to be territorial toward rival males and engage in wrestling, so multiple males in a small tank are generally discouraged. **Data caveat:** Published husbandry data specific to this species is limited; most care parameters are extrapolated from congener (P. pipa, P. parva) care literature and a small number of specialty hobbyist sources. Peer-reviewed aquarium husbandry studies for P. carvalhoi are lacking, so field-tested parameters such as tank size and exact pH and hardness targets carry meaningful uncertainty.
breedingNotes: Like other Pipa, fertilised eggs are pressed into the female's dorsal skin during a series of underwater somersaulting amplexus turnovers; the eggs then sink into honeycomb-like skin pockets. Uniquely among Pipa species, P. carvalhoi retains a free-swimming larval stage — tadpoles hatch from the mother's back after roughly 14–28 (some sources up to 30) days of dermal brooding and then complete development as filter-feeding free-living larvae. Tadpoles are commonly raised on fine powdered spirulina (or spirulina-enriched microbrine shrimp) in bare-bottom rearing containers at roughly 25–28°C with very low water volumes per tadpole (~0.1 L initially, scaling up). Sexual maturity has been reported at around 7–9 months, and captive pairs have produced repeat clutches every 4–8 weeks. Captive breeding is generally considered moderately challenging; tadpole survival past the first week is the most commonly cited bottleneck.

primary.name: Gone Froggin — Carvalho's Surinam Toad (Pipa carvalhoi)
primary.notes: Used as primary because the manifest-supplied Josh's Frogs root returned HTTP 403 and Josh's Frogs stocks Pipa parva (Sabana Suriname Toad) rather than P. carvalhoi. Gone Froggin provides species-specific data on size (males 57 mm / females 68 mm), aquatic lifestyle, male territorial wrestling, back-brooding development (2–4 weeks before tadpole release), inguinal amplexus, and conservation status.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Carvalho's Surinam toad
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pipidae, Anura; described by Miranda-Ribeiro 1937), Brazilian endemism, habitat (dry/moist shrubland, savanna, freshwater marshes, ponds, aquaculture ponds), and IUCN status (Least Concern). Article is a stub — no husbandry data.

additional.name: Frog Forum — Pipa carvalhoi breeding thread
additional.notes: Cross-checked tadpole rearing parameters (temperature 25–28°C, bare-bottom containers, ~0.1 L/tadpole scaling to 0.5 L, powdered spirulina or enriched microbrine shrimp diet). Hobbyist source — not peer-reviewed.

additional.name: AmphibiaWeb — Pipa carvalhoi (manifest-supplied secondary, unreachable)
additional.notes: Manifest-supplied secondary returned a CAPTCHA wall to the fetch tool; substituted Gone Froggin (primary) + Wikipedia + Frog Forum. Search-result snippets from AmphibiaWeb were consistent with the data used (free-swimming tadpole stage; ~14–28 days of dermal brooding; captive clutches every 4–8 weeks; ~44–46 mm SVL specimen records).

additional.name: Josh's Frogs (manifest-supplied primary, unreachable)
additional.notes: Manifest-supplied primary source root. Returned HTTP 403 to the fetch tool. Independent checks indicate Josh's Frogs stocks Pipa parva (Sabana Suriname Toad), not P. carvalhoi, so even a successful fetch would not have provided species-specific husbandry data. Primary role swapped to Gone Froggin per playbook §3 fallback rule.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Females brood eggs in honeycomb-like dorsal skin pockets, but unlike Pipa pipa the offspring emerge as free-swimming tadpoles after roughly 14–28 days rather than as fully-formed froglets. Tadpoles are commonly raised on powdered spirulina at 25–28°C in bare-bottom containers. Captive pairs have been reported producing repeat clutches every 4–8 weeks.
